insights: IT department achieves a 65.12% goal completion rate—24.50 percentage points higher than the lowest-performing department and 20.5–24.5 pp above other departments—driven entirely by its strategic concentration on Critical/High priority goals (85.27% vs 17.81% across others).

Critical/High priority goals have a universally higher completion rate (69.19%) than Medium/Low goals (37.81%), with a consistent 26.34–39.33 pp gap across all departments, confirming priority allocation—not execution capability—is the primary driver of goal achievement differences.

The organization-wide goal completion rate would increase from 48.36% to 64.46% (+16.10 pp) if all departments reallocated their goal portfolios to match IT’s Critical/High priority mix, yielding 89 additional completed goals out of 550.

Finance has the highest improvement potential (+23.84 pp, +58.7% relative), followed by HR (+20.33 pp) and Marketing (+19.87 pp), indicating that priority reallocation delivers disproportionate gains where current Critical/High goal share is lowest.

IT’s median goal duration is 143 days—42 days shorter than the 185-day median for other departments—and Critical/High priority goals consistently exhibit shorter durations (143–173 days) than Medium/Low goals (185 days), suggesting priority-driven focus accelerates goal execution as well as completion.

No significant differences exist between IT and other departments in category, metric, target_percentage, start date, or overall timeline structure—confirming that structural or contextual factors do not explain performance gaps, and reinforcing priority composition as the sole differentiating lever.
summary: The elevated goal achievement rates in the IT department are fully explained by its deliberate concentration on Critical and High priority goals (85.27% of its portfolio), which systematically achieve 31.38 percentage points higher completion rates across the entire organization—regardless of department. This priority-completion effect is universal, robust, and independent of category, metric, target setting, or timeline characteristics. As a result, replicating IT’s priority mix organization-wide would lift the overall completion rate by 16.10 pp (to 64.46%), delivering 89 additional completed goals—with Finance, HR, and Marketing standing to gain the most. These findings shift the focus of goal management from execution support to strategic prioritization governance, and recommend immediate implementation of cross-departmental priority allocation standards and monitoring.
